policy_statement: Incentivize mitigation on private property in the Wildland Urban Interface and provide adequate public funding for forest management on public land.
mechanism_description: The strategy incentivizes wildfire mitigation on private WUI property and provides public funding for forest management on public land to reduce wildfire risk and help preserve forest carbon stocks.
primary_causal_pathway: Forest/WUI management actions → ↓ severe wildfire probability/intensity → ↓ biomass combustion + ↑ forest carbon preservation → ↓ net GHG emissions / ↑ maintained carbon stocks
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Implement defensible space and other mitigation on private WUI parcels and increase public investment in forest management on public lands (e.g., thinning, prescribed burning, fuel breaks, restoration).  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Reduced surface and ladder fuels and improved forest structure lower the chance of crown fires and extreme fire behavior, limiting area burned at high severity and post-fire tree mortality.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Avoided pulse emissions from wildfire combustion and reduced loss of long-lived forest biomass/soil carbon, thereby preserving carbon stocks and sustaining (or restoring) forest carbon sequestration capacity over time.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoiding large wildfire-related CO₂ (and other GHG/aerosol) emissions by reducing severe wildfire occurrence/intensity and preserving forest carbon stocks.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Management interventions reduce severe wildfire behavior, which most directly affects climate via avoided combustion emissions and preserved forest carbon stocks (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although reducing WUI risk lowers human exposure and damages (adaptation), the mechanism explicitly targets forest carbon preservation and avoidance of fire-driven emissions—these are the largest climate-relevant impacts common to the mechanism.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some implementations may focus mainly on protecting structures (home hardening) with minimal carbon benefit, or prescribed burning can create short-term emissions; however, across the mechanism definition (wildfire risk reduction + forest carbon preservation), the intended dominant climate pathway remains net emission avoidance/carbon stock retention.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy fitting this mechanism includes forest/wildfire risk management aimed at preventing high-severity fires and preserving forest carbon, which is inherently a mitigation-oriented pathway.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Specific subtypes (e.g., forestry mitigation vs. resilience/WUI protection emphasis) require policy-level evidence and are not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Policies centered only on WUI property protection (without meaningful forest carbon outcomes) would tilt toward Adaptation, and prescribed fire may raise near-term emissions; classification here assumes the mechanism’s defining intent and effect includes net wildfire-severity reduction and forest carbon preservation.
key_indicators: “Incentivize mitigation on private property”; “Wildland Urban Interface (WUI)”; “provide adequate public funding”; “forest management on public land”
co_benefits: Reduced wildfire risk to life/property; improved air quality from fewer/severe wildfires; ecosystem health/biodiversity benefits; avoided disaster response costs
instance_edge_case_notes: Single statement bundles two instruments (private-property incentives and public-land funding). Classified by the dominant explicit instrument cue (“Incentivize”); public funding component is consistent with the same incentive-program family rather than a separate instrument type.
